Welcome everyone to the math breakdown of the unfair advantage for hybrid. So this is a technical model that most people run, right? They spend $1,000 on ads, they get 100 leads, 70 of them schedule, 35 of them show, 20 of them close at $500 for a front end challenge of some sort, and most gyms are running on a 10-20%-ish margin, okay? And so that means they're making $2,000 from their $1,000 that they put in in profit, which means that their max break-even per lead before they start losing money in the acquisition is about $20 a lead, all right? And up to this point before this, this model beat out the LBO model, the low-barried entry model because they'd be losing so much money on the acquisition that this at least allowed people to break-even or make more on the acquisition than it cost them. Now, this is the hybrid model, and I'm going to use the exact same number, so you spend $1,000, get 100 leads, 70 schedule, 35 show, 20 close, profit is now 90%, all right? And so now you make $9,000, which means your max break-even per lead is $90 a lead. Now, mind you, this break-even cost doesn't even take into account recurring revenue, pay-and-fills, or product sales, all right? So if we do take those into account, the contrast becomes even more pronounced, all right? And so this is an optimized hybrid model from a selling standpoint, all right? So you spend the same thousand, we're going all the way down to where we were before, and now we have 35 people who show. Now let's assume that you make 20 sales, okay? At 500, which is where we were at before. The other 15 people, if you're following our model to the T, should become no-sale sales. And what those are, are people that you're going to ask for a deposit to come back and do a nutritional orientation with you, and you're going to provide a templated model rather than a personalized model for them. You're going to make sure that they come separately, not at the same times as people who've paid, obviously. Now you do that because afterwards, you're going to be able to sell them product, okay? So the first people we're selling product to is the people that we already purchased, obviously the same 20 from before. And on product, you're going to make a 42% margin, which means you add another 1680. Now if that doesn't sound like much, that was literally the entire profit that the old model was even making to begin with, if you remember from just a couple, a minute ago, right? So it is very significant to do that, and that automatically allows you to recoup your ad cost, okay? Which is a really good thing. All right, next, on top of that, your NoCaSell, let's say that of the 15 people who come, 10 of them close, and an average ticket of 315. Now you'll note that it is a higher average ticket for people who purchased, who did not purchase services than people who did. And that, I have no idea why, I'm just telling you the observation that you should note. And then from those people, you make an extra 1323, which also means that from the people who would have said no normally, we have already recouped our ad cost as well. So people who said no are now paying for advertising campaigns for you. It's pretty cool, all right? Now after that point, we know that of all the clients that we have exposed to, at the end of a six-week program, about 20% of people, when giving the offer, will take a pay-in full. A pay-in full for online training is $3,000. So if you have 20% of your 30, that means six people taking a $3,000 offer, it's $18,000 in additional revenue that comes in, and that's just within the first six weeks, okay? So on top of that, the other 80%, because remember, only six of them took the pay-in full, so the other 24 people are going to come in and you will be able to convert them into your EFT, and this is an online EFT, a hybrid EFT, which runs at 90%, plus, and depending on how much you're paying your trainers. If you're the only one doing it, then you can run a 90% margin on it. If you're having trainers to it, then you're going to have to subtract the 20% that you be paying them to service the customers, all right? That being said, either way, you still added $3,600 in recurring revenue at the end of the six-week period, okay? Which means that overall, and this is just profit, remember, because I took all the percentages out, $33,603 is what comes out of this initial campaign, okay? Now these are examples. These are not representative of necessarily what you will achieve. We make no promises, all the legal jargon that you should normally hear here, but I'm giving you just a math example of how this can break down for you. Now this is very significant because if you remember, before this, we had a break-even point of $20 a lead, whereas here we have a break-even point of $336 a lead. For a weight loss customer in a local demographic, that is an incredibly, incredibly high cost relief that you can stomach just on the front end for acquisition, which makes this a very, very difficult model to beat from a math standpoint. And so this is no longer incremental improvement, it's a new model, okay? And so I just want to make sure that this is understood by you of why this is an unfair advantage for you. Inside of this, I combined the product sales, which amounted to $3,000, and also the recurring revenue that comes from those as well. So even in this example, I forgot to mark the second recurring revenue that goes through from the bolt-on supplement sales and the no-sale supplement sales that would also hit before the end of the six weeks, so you'd actually be able to even double that. Now, all of this is making assumptions based on your close percentage and what we have observed over a large group of gyms. All right, so I want you to have confidence in moving into the fact that this is going to be a superior model and that you are going to be able to out-compete your competition, and more importantly, you're going to be able to out-compete your old model, which is what you came here to do. All right, so hope you found this valuable. I think that all of the belief-breaking in the stories and the colorful pictures and whatnot are useful to explain things, but for me, I'm a math person. And so I like knowing why it works so much better, and this is why.
